mismatched_fact_examples.truth_value: SFE results: Base alloy 26.3 ± 3.0 mJ m^-2; 2.5 Si alloy 19.9 ± 2.0 mJ m^-2; 2.5 Al alloy 38.3 ± 6.1 mJ m^-2. Linear SFE coefficients A^SFE found: Si = -2.6 mJ m^-2 wt.%^-1, Al = 4.9 mJ m^-2 wt.%^-1, in good agreement with thermochemical modeling for Al (~5.2 mJ m^-2 wt.%^-1) and high-Mn literature for Si (-1 to -3.5 mJ m^-2 wt.%^-1).
mismatched_fact_examples.prediction_value: Ignoring Al alloying in austenite carbon content calculations based on lattice parameter may overestimate C content by 0.125 to 0.176 wt.% for 1 wt.% Al alloying, depending on the literature A_C^LP value used (3.30 to 4.67×10⁻³ nm wt.%⁻¹).
mismatched_fact_examples.truth_normalized: sfe results: base alloy 26.3 ± 3.0 mj m^-2; 2.5 si alloy 19.9 ± 2.0 mj m^-2; 2.5 al alloy 38.3 ± 6.1 mj m^-2. linear sfe coefficients a^sfe found: si = -2.6 mj m^-2 wt.%^-1, al = 4.9 mj m^-2 wt.%^-1, in good agreement with thermochemical modeling for al (~5.2 mj m^-2 wt.%^-1) and high-mn literature for si (-1 to -3.5 mj m^-2 wt.%^-1).
mismatched_fact_examples.prediction_normalized: ignoring al alloying in austenite carbon content calculations based on lattice parameter may overestimate c content by 0.125 to 0.176 wt.% for 1 wt.% al alloying, depending on the literature a_c^lp value used (3.30 to 4.67x10-3 nm wt.%-1).
mismatched_fact_examples.reason: LLM judged same-field values as not semantically matched.

mismatched_fact_examples.truth_value: Ignoring Al effect on lattice parameter overestimates retained austenite C content by δ_C = x_Al (A_Al^LP / A_C^LP); for 1 wt.% Al, δ_C is estimated at 0.125–0.176 wt.%, which is non-negligible relative to the carbon levels that influence austenite thermodynamic stability.
mismatched_fact_examples.prediction_value: Uncorrected weak-beam dark-field TEM measurements yielded stacking fault energies of 26.3 ± 3.0 mJ/m² for the base alloy, 38.3 ± 6.1 mJ/m² for the 2.5 Al alloy, and 19.9 ± 2.0 mJ/m² for the 2.5 Si alloy. Relative to the base alloy, 2.5 wt.% Al increased SFE by 12.0 mJ/m² and 2.5 wt.% Si decreased it by 6.4 mJ/m².
mismatched_fact_examples.truth_normalized: ignoring al effect on lattice parameter overestimates retained austenite c content by δ_c = x_al (a_al^lp / a_c^lp); for 1 wt.% al, δ_c is estimated at 0.125-0.176 wt.%, which is non-negligible relative to the carbon levels that influence austenite thermodynamic stability.
mismatched_fact_examples.prediction_normalized: uncorrected weak-beam dark-field tem measurements yielded stacking fault energies of 26.3 ± 3.0 mj/m2 for the base alloy, 38.3 ± 6.1 mj/m2 for the 2.5 al alloy, and 19.9 ± 2.0 mj/m2 for the 2.5 si alloy. relative to the base alloy, 2.5 wt.% al increased sfe by 12.0 mj/m2 and 2.5 wt.% si decreased it by 6.4 mj/m2.
mismatched_fact_examples.reason: LLM judged same-field values as not semantically matched.

llm_judge_candidates.truth_value: 2.5 wt.% Al alloying increased the austenite lattice parameter by 1.41 × 10^-3 nm relative to the base alloy, while 2.5 wt.% Si had a minimal effect. Linear lattice parameter coefficients A^LP found: Si = -0.018 × 10^-3 nm wt.%^-1, Al = 0.582 × 10^-3 nm wt.%^-1, consistent with Dyson and Holmes [16].
